claim: Public stillbirth evidence in high-burden countries is fragmented enough across survey, CRVS, and facility sources that a reviewer cannot responsibly rank where apparent stillbirth burden most likely reflects intrapartum care failure versus weak counting without an explicit measure-family reconciliation step.
failure_modes: A contributor could treat facility stillbirth rates as direct quality rankings without accounting for referral case mix and denominator bias.
A contributor could compare stillbirth levels across countries that use different gestational-age or birthweight thresholds and call the result a policy ranking.
A reviewer could over-read the current evidence and assume the pack already proves where quality-of-care investment dominates registration-system investment.
kill_condition: A reviewer demonstrates a current public source set for at least one high-burden country where survey, CRVS, and facility stillbirth measures align on threshold, denominator, time reference, and sub-national grain well enough that reconciliation adds no material decision value.
limitations: This is a claim about public evidence comparability, not about true biological stillbirth risk in the world.
The current evidence records anchor burden and policy framing but do not yet inventory survey, CRVS, and facility sources directly.
